elements.sent-2411-005: This suggests that certain special cosmological states may have a Euclidean path integral construction (similar to Hartle-Hawking) where the gravitational path integral has a holographic definition via a pair of holographic Euclidean CFTs \cite{Maldacena:2004rf,VanRaamsdonk:2020tlr .}

elements.sent-2411-061: In this section, we describe the setup in detail, starting with the final picture of a connected Euclidean path integral giving rise to a purified cosmology.
elements.sent-2411-062: Consider a $d$-dimensional holographic CFT, with a corresponding dual $\Lambda < 0$ gravitational effective field theory that describes the physics in the asymptotically AdS spacetimes dual to the CFT states.
elements.sent-2411-063: We describe a Euclidean path integral construction for a state of many such CFTs whose dual gravitational wavefunction includes a description of approximately homogeneous and isotropic big-bang / big-crunch cosmological spacetimes filled with black holes.
elements.sent-2411-064: Let $B$ be the homogeneous and isotropic geometry (sphere, plane or hyperbolic space) that we want the spatial geometry of our cosmology to approximate.
elements.sent-2411-065: We consider two copies of the Euclidean version of our holographic CFT on $B$.
elements.sent-2411-066: Next, we deform the geometry to introduce a number of tubes connecting the two copies of $B$.
elements.sent-2411-067: Topologically, we remove a collection of $n$ $d$-dimensional balls from each copy of $B$ and then glue each $(d-1)$-sphere boundary to the corresponding boundary on the other copy of $B$.
elements.sent-2411-068: We can take the tubes to be spherically symmetric, characterized by some length and radius.
elements.sent-2411-069: We denote by $B_2$ this CFT geometry with two copies of $B$ connected by tubes.
elements.sent-2411-070: On its own, the CFT path integral on each cylindrical tube constructs a thermofield double state of a pair of CFTs on the boundary spheres.
elements.sent-2411-071: This state can also be constructed by a Euclidean path integral on a pair of disks with a correlated insertions of operators, as shown in Figure (\ref{fig:TFDtube ).}
elements.sent-2411-072: Thus, we expect that for the purpose of evaluating correlators on $B_2$ with no operators inserted on the tubes, it should be equivalent to replace the tubes with correlated insertions of operators on pairs of disks.
elements.sent-2411-073: On the other hand, the tube picture gives us some additional observables (we can insert operators on the tubes), and on the gravity side, gives rise to a more complete geometry.

elements.sent-2411-074: A key point in either description is that we are introducing correlations between the fields in one copy of the CFT and the fields in the other copy of the CFT.
elements.sent-2411-075: These correlations are reflected in the existence of a Euclidean wormhole solution that connects the two copies of $B$ through the bulk\footnote{Technically, this isn't a usual wormhole since the boundary is connected because of the tubes.. .}
elements.sent-2411-076: The wormhole solution we are interested in is one where the bulk geometry topologically fills the region between the two copies of $B$, leaving the tubes unfilled.

elements.sent-2411-349: In this paper, we have described a microscopic construction of a big-bang / big-crunch cosmological spacetime using the tools of holography.
elements.sent-2411-350: We used a Euclidean CFT path integral to define a particular entangled state of a collection of uncoupled CFTs; in some cases, the dual spacetime has an interior region that is a cosmology where the matter is a lattice of black holes.
elements.sent-2411-351: A significant quantitative result is that the saddle giving rise to the cosmology only dominates the path integral for a range of parameters where the size of the black holes and the separation between the horizons is similar to the cosmological scale, $r > r_{crit = 1.12 \ell_{AdS}$.}
elements.sent-2411-352: In this sense, the spacetimes are quite inhomogeneous at the scale of the lattice, but the lattice itself is perfectly homogeneous and isotropic.
elements.sent-2411-353: The results show that a cosmology with black holes that are much smaller than the cosmological scale (as in our own universe) will only arise from the contributions of a subdominant saddle and thus will be a rare part of the wavefunction in our construction.
elements.sent-2411-354: It would be interesting to understand whether the physics of such a cosmology can still be extracted from the dual CFT state in some way or whether there is a projection on the CFT state that projects onto this cosmology on the gravity side.
elements.sent-2411-355: For example, projection of the CFT state to a high-energy band for each CFT (with energies of order $c$) might largely remove the contributions of the non-cosmological spacetimes for which the CFT energies are of order $c^0$.
elements.sent-2411-356: While our explicit calculations have been in three-dimensional gravity, we expect that the general picture should be qualitatively the same in higher-dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-357: For example, the case of a spherical ``lattice'' with two black holes reduces to the thermofield double construction, and here the physics is similar in higher dimensions, with the Hawking-Page transition between the two-sided black hole (the ``cosmological'' solution).
elements.sent-2411-358: Constructing solutions in higher dimensions would generally require numerics, but similar numerical computations have been done in the past (see e.g. \cite{bentivegna2018black ).}
elements.sent-2411-359: A simple analytic construction of various cosmologies with matter and black holes was given in \cite{ Sahu2024Singular .}
elements.sent-2411-360: Here, the idea is to start with a homogeneous and isotropic universe with matter and radiation; from this universe, we can remove spherical spatial regions and patch on an interior region of a Schwarzschild black hole, satisfying the Israel junction conditions via a shell of dust.

elements.subsubsection-2411-012: A closed universe Hilbert space from auxiliary degrees of freedom
elements.sent-2411-369: An interesting feature of our solutions is that while some of them describe ``closed universes,'' e.g. with spherical or toroidal spatial slices, there is still a non-trivial underlying Hilbert space, associated with the second asymptotic regions of the black holes.
elements.sent-2411-370: From the bulk perspective, the cosmology is in a mixed state, purified by fields in the second asymptotic regions.
elements.sent-2411-371: The full states are dual to states in the Hilbert space of the collection of CFTs; here, there are many possible states.
elements.sent-2411-372: Thus, our situation appears to be qualitatively different from other settings e.g. \cite{Usatyuk:2024mzs where it has been argued that the Hilbert space for closed universes is one-dimensional.}
elements.sent-2411-373: While our situation has second asymptotic regions for the black holes and thus is in some sense not completely closed, we might expect something similar in cases without black holes where the matter is in a mixed state, entangled with some external auxiliary degrees of freedom.
elements.sent-2411-374: In our construction, we began by considering a mixed state cosmology generated by a Euclidean path integral for a pair of Euclidean CFTs with an ensemble of operator insertions.
elements.sent-2411-375: We then purified the construction by noting that the ensemble of insertions could be reproduced by introducing a set of auxiliary degrees of freedom (the CFT degrees of freedom on a set of tubes connecting the two CFTs) and integrating these out.
elements.sent-2411-376: This construction corresponded to a natural purification of the cosmology in the Lorentzian picture where we add a second asymptotic region to each black hole.
elements.sent-2411-377: There are many other possible purifications.
elements.sent-2411-378: In the Euclidean picture, this is related to the fact that there are many possible ways to introduce auxiliary degrees of freedom and integrate them out in order to obtain the same ensemble of operator insertions.
elements.sent-2411-379: In various examples, the Lorentzian degrees of freedom associated with the time-symmetric slice of the Euclidean path integral will be different.
elements.sent-2411-380: Thus, the same cosmology can be holographically encoded in many different ways, using many possible sets of degrees of freedom.
elements.sent-2411-381: In the various examples, the spacetime past the horizons of the black holes will be different, but physics in the cosmological region will be the same.
elements.sent-2411-382: The idea that the same spacetime can be encoded using various holographic descriptions was discussed previously in \cite{van2020spacetime,VanRaamsdonk2018,simidzija_holo-ween_2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-383: In cases where the cosmological saddle is dominant, it is interesting to understand how various regions of the cosmology are encoded in the dual CFTs.
elements.sent-2411-384: Consider the case where we have a planar lattice of black holes.
elements.sent-2411-385: Consider the density matrix associated with a collection of CFTs associated with a connected region containing $n$ black holes.
elements.sent-2411-386: A candidate RT surface for the entanglement entropy of this collection of CFTs is the set of $n$ horizons homologous to the $n$ asymptotic boundaries corresponding to the CFTs.
elements.sent-2411-388: However, we can consider an alternative surface in the cosmological region of the geometry that surrounds all of these horizons.
elements.sent-2411-389: The length of this can be upper bounded by $mA$, where $A$ is the distance shown in figure 5 (generally also of order $L$).
elements.sent-2411-390: This will certainly be smaller than the other candidate surface when \begin{equation}mA < nL \; .\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-439: Riemann surfaces with $g \geq 2$ admit a discrete group of automorphisms.
elements.sent-2411-440: In mathematical literature, these groups have been classified using the properties of the hyperelliptic functions (complex equation of the form $y^2=p(x)$) corresponding to a Riemann surface.
elements.sent-2411-441: Hurwitz's theorem provides an upper bound of $84(g-1)$ on the order of the automorphism group for connected Riemann surfaces with $g\geq2$.
elements.sent-2411-442: This upper bound is not always reached however; in case of $g=2$, the biggest automorphism group is of order $48$ and is called a Bolza curve.
elements.sent-2411-443: We are interested in a two dimensional moduli space of genus 2 Riemann surfaces where the surface has $\mathbb{Z _2 \times \mathbb{Z _2 \times \mathbb{Z _2$ symmetry.}}}
elements.sent-2411-444: This 8-fold Abelian group of symmetries is often enhanced at particular points in the moduli space.
elements.sent-2411-445: In this section we want to understand how this enhancement occurs.
elements.sent-2411-446: A general $g=2$ surface has a moduli space of $3$ complex or $6$ real dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-447: When such a surface has a $\mathbb{Z _2$ symmetry, the number of parameters reduces to $3$, which correspond to the lengths of 3 circles fixed by this $\mathbb{Z _2$ symmetry.}}
elements.sent-2411-449: Making 2 of these A-cycles equal in length, introduces an additional $\mathbb{Z _2$ symmetry that swaps the two A-cycles of equal length.}
elements.sent-2411-450: It is slightly non-trivial to see how, but having two of the $\mathbb{Z _2$ symmetries ensures a third $\mathbb{Z _2$ symmetry which fixes a second set of B-cycles \footnote{ The explanation for this is related to the fact that all the genus 2 hypersurfaces under consideration are associated with a hyperelliptic curve of the form $y^2=p(x)$ where $p$ is a polynomial of degree $5$ or $6$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-451: Then the involution $y\xrightarrow{ -y$ gives a Riemann surface in which the roles of the A-cycles and the B-cycles are interchanged.}
elements.sent-2411-452: Thus we have a different set of moduli, but they correspond to the same genus 2 Riemann surface.
elements.sent-2411-453: It is similar to the genus 1 case where interchanging the 'contractible' and 'non-contractible' cycles gives a different bulk solution, but the boundary is the same.
elements.sent-2411-454: Composition of the involution and one of the $\mathbb{Z _2$ gives rise to a new $\mathbb{Z _2$.. .}}

elements.sent-2411-473: We found that a genus 2 Riemann surface with $\mathbb{Z _2 \times \mathbb{Z _2 \times \mathbb{Z _2$ symmetry and a hyperbolic metric may be constructed by joining hyperbolic right angled pentagons.}}}
elements.sent-2411-474: Such pentagons have only two free parameters; fixing the length of any two adjacent sides fixes the length of the rest of the sides.
elements.sent-2411-475: Let two adjacent sides of the pentagon be $a,b$ and the unique side non-adjacent to both these sides be of length $c$.
elements.sent-2411-476: Then they satisfy the relation \begin{equation}\label{eq:pentagon_1} \cosh c = \sinh a \sinh b,\end{equation} which of course holds for any pair of adjacent sides.
elements.sent-2411-477: Let the two remaining sides be of lengths $m$, $n$ where the former corresponds to the side between the sides of length $a$ and $c$.
elements.sent-2411-478: Then the following relation also holds: \begin{equation}\sinh{m}\cosh{a}=\cosh{b}\sinh{n}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-479: This relation involves 4 adjacent sides and it also holds for any 4 adjacent sides.
elements.sent-2411-480: This set of relations is not independent from the set of relations exemplified by \cref{eq:pentagon_1 ; one set of relations can be derived from the other.}
elements.sent-2411-481: Another set of relations involving 3 adjacent sides is \begin{equation}\tanh{m}\cosh{c}\tanh{n} = 1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-482: Explicitly, the sides $m,n$ are given in terms of $a,b$ by \begin{equation}\cosh{m} = \frac{\cosh a \sinh b}{\sqrt{\sinh^2 a \sinh^2 b - 1}} \cosh{n} = \frac{\cosh b \sinh a}{\sqrt{\sinh^2 a \sinh^2 b - 1}}.\end{equation}.
